prompt: I'm building a practice management tool for solo therapists. Each therapist signs up and can add their own client list, schedule sessions, write private session notes, and send invoices that clients pay by card. Therapists should only ever see their own clients and notes. The 39 dollar a month plan unlocks unlimited clients and card payments; the free plan caps them at 5 clients.

I want a tool for wedding photographers to deliver photos to their couples. Each photographer creates client galleries, uploads hundreds of photos, and shares a private link with the couple so they can view and download originals. Photographers only see their own galleries; couples should only access the gallery they were invited to. The pro plan at 25 dollars a month removes the 2GB upload limit.

Building an invoicing app for freelance designers. Each designer adds their clients, sends branded invoices, and tracks who has paid. Designers only see their own invoices and clients. The 15 dollar a month plan unlocks recurring invoices and removes our logo from invoices; free users are capped at 3 active clients.

I want a CRM for solo real estate agents. Each agent uploads their listings with photos, tracks which leads are interested in which property, and logs every showing with notes about the buyer. Agents only see their own listings and leads. A 49 dollar a month plan unlocks unlimited listings; free is capped at 5.

Building a newsletter platform for independent writers. Each writer signs up, builds their own subscriber list, and writes posts that go out by email. Some posts are free and some are paid-subscriber only at whatever price the writer sets. We take a 10 percent cut on every paid subscription that flows through the platform.

I'm building a scheduling tool for music teachers who teach kids. Each teacher adds their students with parent contact info, schedules weekly lessons, and parents pay tuition through the app. Teachers only see their own students. A 20 dollar a month plan unlocks SMS lesson reminders and unlimited students; the free tier is capped at 10 students.

Building software for solo wedding planners. Each planner adds their weddings with vendor lists, budgets, guest lists, and dietary requirements. Planners only see the weddings they're managing. The couple gets a read-only login that shows them their own wedding details and nothing else. A 60 dollar a month plan unlocks unlimited weddings; free caps at 2.

I need an expense reimbursement tool for our 80-person startup. Employees log in with their work email, snap a photo of a receipt, and submit it for approval. Their direct manager sees only their own team's submissions and can approve or reject. Finance staff can see everything and mark things as paid.

Internal PTO tracking for our company. Employees request days off, see their remaining balance, and the request routes to their manager. Managers approve or deny and can see who on their team is out which week. HR can see everyone in the company and override any decision a manager made.

Internal tool for tracking laptops and equipment we issue to employees. IT staff can add equipment, assign it to an employee, and mark it returned. Every employee can see what's assigned to them but nobody else's assignments. Only IT admins can edit equipment records or reassign anything.

I want a visitor check-in app for our office lobby on an iPad. Visitors type their name and who they're meeting, and the employee gets a notification. Reception can see today's full visitor list. Security admins can pull historical visitor logs going back a full year and export them.

Internal tool for our recruiting team. Employees submit referrals for open roles and attach the candidate's resume. Recruiters review submissions and update status as interviewing, hired, or rejected. Employees can only see the status of people they personally referred. Recruiting admins can see every submission and export the full dataset.

I want a sales analytics dashboard for my Shopify store. It pulls in orders from Shopify, ad spend from Facebook and Google, and shows my real profit per product after ad costs. Only me and my two co-founders should be able to log in and view it. We connect our Shopify and ad accounts once during setup.

Dashboard for restaurant owners with multiple locations. Each owner sees nightly sales, food costs, and labor costs broken down by location. Store managers log in and only see their one location's numbers. The owner can also see who's clocked in across every location in real time.

A studio owner dashboard for boutique fitness studios. The owner sees class attendance, membership renewals, and which instructors are booked solid. Each instructor logs in and sees only their own upcoming classes and student rosters. The owner can also pull a list of members whose cards on file are about to expire.

Dashboard for solar panel installation companies. The dispatcher sees every install crew's live location, today's jobs, and how each install is progressing through uploaded photos. Crew leads only see their own job assignments. The customer we're installing for gets a private link to track progress on their specific install and nothing else.

A dashboard for landlords with rental properties. The landlord sees every unit, who lives there, when rent was paid, and open maintenance requests. Tenants log in to see only their own unit, pay rent, and submit maintenance tickets. Maintenance staff see all the open tickets across properties but never any rent payment info.

A plant care reminder app for plant parents. Users sign up, add their plants with photos, and get notifications when it's time to water or fertilize. They can mark each plant as private or share it publicly on their profile for other users to see. People can follow other plant parents whose collections they like.

A workout tracking app for gym goers. People log their lifts, see their progress over time, and can choose which specific workouts to share with friends. Their friend list is private and only they can see who follows them. There's a 5 dollar a month tier that unlocks AI-generated workout plans tailored to their history.

A book club app for friend groups. Someone creates a book club, invites friends by email, and the group picks a book to read together. Only invited members of a club can see that club's discussions and reading progress. Each member can post chapter reactions that only their club sees and nobody else.

A local farmers market app. Shoppers browse stalls at nearby markets and see what each vendor is selling that week. Vendors sign up separately, list their products with photos, and update what's in stock day to day. Vendors can only edit their own listings; shoppers can favorite stalls but cannot post anything publicly.

I want a tool that summarizes contracts for small business owners. They upload a contract PDF and the AI flags risky clauses, payment terms, and termination conditions in plain English. Our prompts contain a proprietary risk scoring rubric we tuned for months and absolutely do not want competitors copying. Users pay 30 dollars a month for unlimited contract reviews.

An AI resume rewriter for job seekers. They paste in their current resume and the job description they're targeting, and the AI rewrites the resume to match. We have a carefully tuned set of instructions about formatting and keyword optimization that took weeks to dial in and is our entire moat. Free users get 3 rewrites; the 12 dollar a month plan is unlimited.

I want an AI customer support widget that ecommerce stores embed on their site. Each store owner signs up, pastes in their FAQ and return policy, and gets a chat widget that answers customer questions using AI. Every store's data and custom instructions must be totally isolated from every other store. Stores pay 49 dollars a month per widget.

An AI homework helper for kids ages 8 to 14. Parents create an account, add their kids, and the kids chat with an AI tutor that helps them work through problems without ever giving away the answer. We have strict instructions telling the AI never to discuss inappropriate topics with children no matter what the kid asks. Parents pay 15 dollars a month per child.
